rationale_for_baseline_tier: An acutely and severely inflamed ear that could represent a can't-miss bacterial process (perichondritis, mastoiditis, malignant otitis externa) requires urgent same-day in-person assessment (T3) — a telehealth service cannot examine the ear, palpate the mastoid or perform otoscopy to safely exclude these. The history (earlobe sparing, afebrile, no discharge, no trauma, prior episodes) strongly favours a benign autoimmune flare, which is why this is NOT a T4/T5 emergency; but it is more urgent than a routine or 24-48 hour GP appointment because the dangerous mimics must be excluded promptly. If in-person review confirms a mild flare, ongoing care reverts to GP/rheumatology within 24-48 hours per the source plan.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness spreading down onto the neck or face
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Attend an emergency department immediately if this develops
escalation_edges.rationale: Spread beyond the cartilage boundary suggests progressing cellulitis/erysipelas or invasive infection needing IV antibiotics and ED assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness starts spreading down onto your neck or face, go to the nearest emergency department straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe deep throbbing pain behind the ear over the mastoid bone, or a new high fever
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Attend an emergency department immediately if this develops
escalation_edges.rationale: Post-auricular/mastoid pain or high fever raises acute mastoiditis or invasive infection, which carry a risk of intracranial spread and need emergency care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a severe deep throbbing pain behind your ear over the bone, or a high fever, go to the emergency department immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Thick yellow pus draining from inside the ear canal, or a sudden drop in hearing
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek urgent same-day medical review if this develops
escalation_edges.rationale: Purulent discharge or hearing loss points to otitis externa/media or invasive infection rather than isolated auricular chondritis and needs urgent in-person assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If thick yellow pus starts draining from inside the ear, or your hearing suddenly drops, get seen urgently the same day.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm the patient can get themselves to an in-person assessment and can monitor for the escalation triggers overnight; provide a clear plan for who to call if symptoms worsen while alone.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Your ear needs to be looked at in person today. Please arrange an urgent same-day appointment with a GP, urgent care clinic or ENT so someone can examine the ear, check behind it and look in the ear canal — this is to make sure it isn't a serious infection. In the meantime, use a cold pack wrapped in cloth on the ear for about 15 minutes every couple of hours, keep all pressure off it, and do not apply heat.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call your GP or an urgent care clinic today to be seen in person; use after-hours/locum services if it is out of hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours, contact an after-hours GP or urgent care service for same-day review, or attend an emergency department if any of the escalation warning signs appear.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note the sudden onset, the earlobe-sparing distribution, afebrile status, absence of discharge/trauma, and the history of prior cartilage flares; these support a relapsing-polychondritis flare and guide whether NSAIDs or a short corticosteroid course are appropriate.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating an acutely inflamed ear as routine (self-care or GP in 1-2 weeks) and failing to arrange prompt in-person exclusion of bacterial perichondritis/mastoiditis.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A missed bacterial perichondritis or mastoiditis progresses to cartilage necrosis, permanent deformity, or intracranial spread.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Deferring to a 24-48 hour GP appointment without recognising that same-day examination is preferable while the dangerous mimics remain unexcluded.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a well, afebrile patient with a classic mild flare to the emergency department or calling an ambulance, over-consuming emergency resources.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot examine the ear to confirm the cartilage-confined pattern or exclude bacterial perichondritis, mastoiditis and otitis externa.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Hold a strong provisional impression of an autoimmune flare while arranging urgent same-day in-person examination and giving explicit escalation triggers.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either confidently diagnosing and discharging over chat, or over-reacting with 000/ambulance for a well, afebrile patient.
